questions.question: Explain the difference between user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Also mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: User-level threads are managed by thread library in user space; kernel-level threads are managed directly by OS kernel
User-level threads: advantage - fast creation/switching, disadvantage - cannot utilize multiple processors
Kernel-level threads: advantage - can achieve true parallelism on multiprocessors, disadvantage - slower creation/context switching
Examples: pthread for user-level, Windows kernel threads for kernel-level

questions.question: What is multithreading? List any three benefits of multithreaded programming in operating systems.
keyPoints: Multithreading allows a single process to have multiple flows of execution
Benefits: 1) Better CPU utilization on multicore systems
2) Improved program responsiveness (UI stays active during I/O)
3) Efficient resource sharing (threads share memory within process)
4) Simplified program structure for naturally parallel tasks

questions.question: Describe the three main threading models with a brief note on the advantages and disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: Many-to-One: Multiple user threads mapped to one kernel thread - efficient but no parallelism
One-to-One: Each user thread has its own kernel thread - true parallelism but higher overhead
Many-to-Many: Multiple user threads to multiple kernel threads - balanced approach combining benefits
Advantages/disadvantages vary based on application requirements and system architecture

questions.question: Explain the thread life cycle with the help of the different states a thread can be in.
keyPoints: NEW/BORN: Thread is created but not yet started
READY: Thread is waiting in the ready queue for CPU allocation
RUNNING: Thread is currently executing on the CPU
BLOCKED/WAITING: Thread is waiting for I/O, resource, or another thread
TERMINATED/ZOMBIE: Thread has completed execution but resources not yet reclaimed
State transitions are managed by the OS scheduler

questions.question: What is thread synchronization? Why is it important in multithreaded programs? Give one example of a synchronization problem.
keyPoints: Thread synchronization coordinates access to shared resources by multiple threads
Important to prevent race conditions where outcome depends on timing
Without synchronization: two threads incrementing same variable may lose updates
Example: Two threads writing to same memory location simultaneously
Solutions: mutexes, semaphores, monitors, locks

questions.question: What is thread pooling? Explain its advantages in multithreaded programming.
keyPoints: Thread pooling creates a fixed number of threads in advance to handle tasks
Tasks are submitted to a queue; idle threads pick up tasks from queue
Advantages: 1) Avoids overhead of creating/destroying threads for each task
2) Limits maximum concurrent threads preventing resource exhaustion
3) Improves response time for frequently occurring tasks
4) Better system stability under heavy load

questions.question: Differentiate between concurrency and parallelism in the context of multithreading.
keyPoints: Concurrency: multiple tasks make progress over overlapping time periods (single or multi-CPU)
Parallelism: simultaneous execution on multiple CPU cores/processors
Concurrency can exist on single CPU through time-slicing; parallelism requires multiple cores
Concurrency deals with managing multiple tasks; parallelism deals with executing multiple tasks at once
Modern systems achieve both: multiple threads running concurrently with some executing in parallel

questions.question: What are the different components of a thread? Explain what information is stored in a Thread Control Block (TCB). Also discuss how context switching differs between threads and processes.
keyPoints: Thread components: program counter, registers, stack, thread ID, state, priority
TCB contains: Thread ID, program counter, register values, stack pointer, scheduling info, signal mask, thread-local storage
Context switching between threads is faster: only PC, registers, stack pointer need saving
Process context switch requires saving entire address space information including memory mappings
Thread switching does not require changing memory management unit (MMU) structures
Shared resources between threads reduce switching overhead but require synchronization

questions.question: Discuss thread cancellation in operating systems. Explain the two types of thread cancellation and the challenges involved in implementing thread cancellation safely.
keyPoints: Thread cancellation: terminating a thread before it completes execution
Two types: 1) Asynchronous cancellation - OS terminates thread immediately
2) Deferred cancellation - thread periodically checks for cancellation request
Challenges: 1) Shared resources may be left in inconsistent state
2) Cleanup handlers may not execute
3) System resources held by cancelled thread may not be released
4) Cannot safely cancel while holding locks (may cause deadlock for other threads)
Most systems use deferred cancellation as safer approach

questions.question: Write a detailed note on POSIX threads (pthread). Include discussions on basic pthread functions, thread creation, termination, and synchronization mechanisms provided by pthread.
keyPoints: POSIX threads (pthread) are standardized thread API for UNIX/Linux systems
Thread creation: pthread_create() - creates new thread with specified attributes
Thread termination: pthread_exit() - terminates calling thread, pthread_join() - waits for thread to complete
Synchronization: pthread_mutex_init/lock/unlock/destroy for mutual exclusion
pthread_cond_init/wait/signal/broadcast for condition variables
pthread_rwlock for reader-writer locks
Semaphore operations: sem_init/wait/post/destroy
Thread attributes: detach state, scheduling policy, stack size, priority

questions.question: Explain the concept of thread pools. Design and describe a thread pool architecture. Also discuss when thread pools should be used and what considerations are important while designing a thread pool.
keyPoints: Thread pool: pre-created worker threads waiting to execute tasks from a queue
Architecture: 1) Thread pool manager maintains thread collection
2) Task queue holds work items submitted by producers
3) Worker threads retrieve and execute tasks
When to use: 1) Large number of short-lived tasks
2) Predictable load patterns requiring quick response
3) Limiting resource consumption
Design considerations: 1) Pool size (too small = underutilization, too large = overhead)
2) Queue size and overflow handling
3) Thread lifecycle management (idle timeout, dynamic resizing)
4) Task distribution strategy (work stealing, priority queuing)
